1: Chapter 8 contains ten oracles of restoration (vv2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 14, 19, 20, 23), each introduced by 'Thus says the LORD of hosts.' This structural pattern — the repeated messenger formula — emphasizes the divine authority behind each promise. The chapter directly reverses the six curses of Zechariah 7:10-14: judgment and desolation (Zechariah 7) become restoration and joy (Zechariah 8). The ten-oracle pattern may reflect the ten plagues (reversing judgment into blessing) or simply the common use of ten as a number of completeness.
2: 'I am zealous for Zion with great zeal, and I am zealous for her with great wrath.' The intensity of YHWH's zeal for Jerusalem is double: zeal for Zion and anger against her enemies — both expressed with the same root qana (to be zealous, H7065). This verse echoes Zechariah 1:14-15: the same divine passion that was the ground of the night visions' promises is restated as the ground of the restoration oracles. YHWH's zeal for his city is not diminished by the exile; it is intensified by the nations' complacency (Zechariah 1:15).
3: 'I have returned to Zion and will dwell in the midst of Jerusalem, and Jerusalem shall be called the faithful city, and the mountain of the LORD of hosts, the holy mountain.' Three titles for restored Jerusalem: (1) ir ha'emet (the faithful/truthful city) — the city that was once characterized by lying officials (Zechariah 7:10) becomes characterized by faithfulness; (2) har YHWH tzvaot (the mountain of the LORD of hosts) — the universal Zion theology of Isaiah 2:2-4, Micah 4:1-3; (3) har haqodesh (the holy mountain) — the dwelling of YHWH's holiness in the midst of the city. The transformation of Jerusalem from corrupt capital to holy city is the eschatological promise that the NT applies to the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2).
4: 'Old men and old women shall again sit in the streets of Jerusalem, each with staff in hand because of great age.' The demographic portrait of restored Jerusalem: the very old (staff-using elderly) and the very young (boys and girls playing in the streets) coexist in safety. This is the inverse of the siege and exile: in the Babylonian destruction, the elderly and children were the most vulnerable and most likely to die or be taken (Lamentations 2:20-21; Zechariah 5:11-14). Their return to public space is the sign of shalom — when the vulnerable are safe in public, the society is at peace.
5: 'And the streets of the city shall be full of boys and girls playing in its streets.' The children playing (mesahaqim, from sachaq = to laugh, play, H7832) are the sign of normalcy restored. In ancient cities under threat, children did not play in public — they were kept inside for safety. Playing children in the streets is the biblical image of total peace: Isaiah 11:8 (the nursing child shall play over the hole of the cobra); Jeremiah 30:19 (from them shall come songs of thanksgiving and voices of those who celebrate). The eschatological city is characterized by the safety of the most vulnerable.
6: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: If it is marvelous in the eyes of the remnant of this people in those days, should it also be marvelous in my eyes, declares the LORD of hosts?' The challenge to human incredulity: what seems impossible (yippaleh, H6381 — too wonderful/marvelous, beyond ordinary capacity) to the remnant is not impossible to YHWH. The word pele (wonder, miracle) is used for YHWH's Exodus acts (Exodus 15:11; Psalms 78:12) — the impossible has happened before in Israel's history. The remnant's disbelief in restoration is understandable; YHWH's ability is not limited by their experience.
7: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: Behold, I will save my people from the east country and from the west country.' The ingathering from both east (eretz mizrach hashamesh = land of the rising sun) and west (eretz mevo hashamesh = land of the setting sun) is the universal compass of the return from exile. Not just from Babylon in the east but from every direction — the prophetic vision of ingathering expands beyond the historical return from Persia to a universal eschatological gathering of the scattered people. The NT fulfillment: Matthew 8:11 (many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven).
8: 'And I will bring them to dwell in the midst of Jerusalem. And they shall be my people, and I will be their God, in faithfulness and in righteousness.' The covenant-restoration formula: vehayu li le'am va'ani ehyeh lahem lElohim (they shall be my people and I will be their God). This is the fundamental covenant formula of Sinai (Exodus 6:7; Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 7:23; Zechariah 11:4; Zechariah 30:22; Zechariah 31:33; Zechariah 32:38; Ezekiel 36:28; Zechariah 37:27) — the broken covenant restored. The qualifiers be'emet uvitzedaqah (in faithfulness and in righteousness) describe both YHWH's character in restoration and the quality of the restored relationship.
9: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: Let your hands be strong, you who in these days have been hearing these words from the mouths of the prophets who were present on the day that the foundation of the house of the LORD of hosts was laid, that the temple might be built.' The encouragement to strengthen hands (techezaqna yedeikhem, the same command as Haggai 2:4) is addressed to the temple-building generation. The contrast with the pre-building period (vv10-11: wages were few, enemies many) and the promise of the new period (v12: the vine shall give its fruit) grounds the exhortation: YHWH's past abandonment of the people and his present blessing of their work.
10: 'For before those days there was no wage for man or any wage for beast, neither was there any safety from the foe for him who went out or came in, for I set every man against his neighbor.' The pre-building desolation (520-516 BCE era of Haggai's early oracles, Haggai 1:6-11; Zechariah 2:15-17) was divine discipline: economic futility and social conflict. The word shalach (I set every man against his neighbor) implies divine orchestration of the social conflict — covenant curse producing covenant breakdown. The reversal in v12 is correspondingly comprehensive.
11: 'But now I will not deal with the remnant of this people as in the former days, declares the LORD of hosts.' The pivotal 'but now' (ve'atah lo ... kiyamim harishonim) — the turning point between the old dispensation of judgment and the new dispensation of blessing. The 'former days' are the period of covenantal discipline that produced the exile and the post-exilic economic hardship. The new period is characterized by YHWH's positive relationship with the remnant.
12: 'For there shall be a sowing of peace. The vine shall give its fruit, and the ground shall give its produce, and the heavens shall give their dew. And I will cause the remnant of this people to possess all these things.' The 'sowing of peace' (zera hashalom) — either a seed of peace planted, or a peaceful/fruitful sowing season. The list of natural blessings (vine, ground, heavens, dew) reverses Haggai 1:10-11 (I called for a drought on the land... it withheld its produce). The covenant blessing cycle of Deuteronomy 28:3-5 (blessed in field, vine, sky, land) is restored. The remnant will inherit all these blessings.
13: 'And as you have been a byword of cursing among the nations, O house of Judah and house of Israel, so will I save you, and you shall be a blessing. Fear not, but let your hands be strong.' The reversal of covenant curse to covenant blessing: Israel was used as a curse-formula among the nations ('may you become like Israel!') — now it will become a blessing-formula (cf. Isaiah 65:16). The pairing of Judah and Israel — both houses of the divided kingdom — is the eschatological reunification that Ezekiel 37:15-23 promises: the two sticks become one in YHWH's hand.
14: 'For thus says the LORD of hosts: As I purposed to bring disaster to you when your fathers provoked me to anger, and I did not relent, says the LORD of hosts.' The divine constancy that executed judgment is the same divine constancy that will now execute blessing. The un-relenting consistency of YHWH (he did not relent from judgment, Lamentations 2:17) becomes the guarantee of blessing: the same determination that brought disaster will bring restoration. YHWH's purposes stand — both for judgment and for blessing.
15: 'So again have I purposed in these days to bring good to Jerusalem and to the house of Judah; fear not.' The parallel structure: as YHWH purposed disaster in response to covenant violation, he now purposes good (hetavoti) in response to the covenant's restoration promise. The theological point: YHWH's blessing is not a reversal of his character (as if he changed his mind) but the fulfillment of his covenant commitment, which always included both curse and blessing. The two-sided character of YHWH's covenantal commitment means both the judgment and the restoration were always his purpose.
16: 'These are the things that you shall do: Speak the truth to one another; render in your gates judgments that are true and make for peace.' The ethical imperatives for the restored community: (1) emet dabru (speak truth) — the opposite of the lying leaders of Zechariah 7:10; (2) mishpat emet veshalom shafetu (render judgments of truth and peace in your gates) — the gate was the site of legal proceedings in ancient Israel (Ruth 4:1-11; Amos 5:10-15). The legal system restored to truthful adjudication is the social foundation of the restored city.
17: 'Do not devise evil in your hearts against one another, and love no false oath, for all these things I hate, declares the LORD.' The two prohibitions correspond to the covenant command of Zechariah 7:10 (let none devise evil in his heart against another): internal malice and external perjury. Hate is strong covenant language (cf. Proverbs 6:16-19: seven things YHWH hates; Amos 5:21: I hate, I despise your feasts) — YHWH's moral opposition is absolute. The restoration community's ethical character must reflect the city's new name: ir ha'emet (the faithful city, v3).
18: 'And the word of the LORD of hosts came to me, saying.' The introduction to the oracle about the fasts (vv19-23) completes the response to the Bethel delegation's question of 7:3 about the fifth-month fast. YHWH's answer has taken Zechariah 7-8 to develop: the negative (fasting done for self, not YHWH, is worthless) leads to the positive (the fasts will become feasts of joy when YHWH restores).
19: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: The fast of the fourth month and the fast of the fifth and the fast of the seventh and the fast of the tenth shall be to the house of Judah seasons of joy and gladness and cheerful feasts. Therefore love truth and peace.' The four fasts that commemorated the stages of Jerusalem's fall are transformed into feasts: (1) the fourth-month fast (17 Tammuz = day the city wall was breached, 2 Kings 25:3-4; Jeremiah 39:2); (2) the fifth-month fast (9 Ab = day the temple was burned, 2 Kings 25:8-9); (3) the seventh-month fast (Gedaliah's assassination, 2 Kings 25:25); (4) the tenth-month fast (10 Tevet = when the siege of Jerusalem began, 2 Kings 25:1). The four memorial fasts of national disaster become four festivals of national joy — the grief of destruction transformed into celebration of restoration.
20: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: Peoples shall yet come, even the inhabitants of many cities.' The eschatological vision of vv20-23: the nations streaming to Jerusalem is the Zion eschatology of Isaiah 2:2-4; Micah 4:1-3. Unlike those passages, which describe nations going up to the mountain, Zechariah's vision shows the nations approaching Jews in the streets and asking to accompany them: 'Let us go with you, for we have heard that God is with you' (v23). The universal recognition of YHWH through his people.
21: 'The inhabitants of one city shall go to another, saying, Let us go at once to entreat the favor of the LORD and to seek the LORD of hosts; I myself am going.' The urgency of the seeking nations: holekh gam ani (I myself am going/already going) — the individual declaration that joins the corporate movement. The seeking of YHWH's favor (lechalot et penei YHWH, to entreat the face of YHWH) is the same vocabulary as the Bethel delegation's question (Zechariah 7:2). The nations do spontaneously what the Bethel delegation did deliberately — seek YHWH.
22: 'Many peoples and strong nations shall come to seek the LORD of hosts in Jerusalem and to entreat the favor of the LORD.' The expansion from one city (v20-21) to amim rabbim vegoyim atzumim (many peoples and strong nations) — including powerful nations, not just weak ones seeking protection. The nations' seeking of YHWH in Jerusalem is the fulfillment of Solomon's prayer at the temple dedication (1 Kings 8:41-43: when the foreigner comes and prays toward this house, hear from heaven).
23: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: In those days ten men from the nations of every tongue shall take hold of the robe of a Jew, saying, Let us go with you, for we have heard that God is with you.' The famous 'ten men per Jew' verse: ten (asarah anashim = ten men — perhaps representing a minyan, the quorum for Jewish prayer) from every tongue/language clinging to the robe of a Jew, following him to Jerusalem. The nations' perception that God is with Israel ('we have heard that God is with you', shama'nu ki Elohim imakhem) is the reversal of the exile's scandal: the exile made it appear that YHWH had abandoned Israel; the restoration makes it clear that YHWH is with them. The NT fulfillment: Gentiles joining themselves to the Jewish Messiah and his Jewish apostles (Acts 2:41, 47; Zechariah 11:21; Zechariah 14:27).
